enrichment.short_description: Memos is an open-source, self-hosted, Markdown-native note-taking app built for quick, lightweight capture of notes, ideas and daily logs.
enrichment.seo_title: Memos Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Memos review: an open-source, self-hosted Markdown note-taking app. See features, deployment options, free pricing, and top alternatives for 2026.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Memos (usememos) is an open-source, self-hosted note-taking application designed for fast, low-friction capture of short notes, ideas, links and daily logs. The project is developed by the usememos organization on GitHub, with its first public releases appearing in 2022. Memos is released under the MIT license, meaning it is free to use, modify and self-host with no subscription fees, per-seat charges or vendor lock-in. The backend is written in Go and compiles to a single lightweight binary, distributed as a roughly 20MB Docker image that can run on a Raspberry Pi, a VPS, or any cloud server. It supports SQLite, MySQL or PostgreSQL as its data store, giving users flexibility from a single-user hobby deployment up to larger self-hosted team instances. Architecturally, Memos exposes both REST and gRPC APIs, which lets developers build integrations, browser extensions, or mobile clients on top of the core note store. The frontend is a progressive web app with offline support, dark mode, and a timeline-based interface that resembles a personal microblog rather than a traditional folder-based notes app. Core features include Markdown-native editing with syntax highlighting, table support and LaTeX math rendering, drag-and-drop attachments for images, video, audio and documents, full-text search, a flexible tagging system for organizing notes, and one-click export to Markdown, JSON or CSV so notes always remain portable. Users can selectively share individual memos via public links while keeping the rest of their notes private, and the zero-telemetry design means no usage data is sent to any third party. Because Memos is entirely self-hosted, target users are privacy-conscious individuals, developers, and small teams who want a lightweight alternative to cloud note services like Notion, Evernote or Google Keep without recurring costs or data-ownership concerns. Its closest open-source competitors include Trilium Notes, Joplin, Anytype and Flatnotes, while on the commercial side it is often compared to Notion and Obsidian. Memos differentiates itself through its extreme simplicity: it deliberately avoids the complex nested-page hierarchies of Notion in favor of a flat, tag-driven, timeline-first workflow optimized for quick daily capture rather than long-form document authoring. Deployment simplicity is another differentiator, since a single Docker command is typically enough to get a working instance running, and the project maintains an active GitHub community that contributes translations, themes and client integrations. Because there is no vendor and no company behind Memos in the traditional SaaS sense, pricing is not applicable beyond the cost of the server or hosting the user chooses to run it on.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Memos is a free, open-source note-taking application designed for people who want full control over their notes without relying on a cloud subscription service. Instead of a traditional folder-and-document structure, Memos presents notes as a reverse-chronological timeline, similar to a personal microblog, making it well suited to capturing short-form thoughts, links, and daily logs quickly.

The project is self-hosted, meaning users run their own instance on a server, VPS, home lab device such as a Raspberry Pi, or a cloud container platform. Because the entire codebase is MIT-licensed and available on GitHub, anyone can inspect, modify or extend it, and there is no dependency on an external company staying in business to keep the notes accessible.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: At its core, Memos supports Markdown formatting with syntax highlighting, tables and LaTeX math, along with drag-and-drop attachments for images, video, audio and documents. A flexible tagging system and full-text search make it easy to retrieve older notes, while public sharing links let users publish individual memos without exposing their entire notebook.

Technically, Memos ships as a single Go binary and a compact Docker image, supports SQLite, MySQL or PostgreSQL as the storage backend, and exposes REST and gRPC APIs for building custom integrations. The interface is a progressive web app with offline support and a dark mode, and all data can be exported to Markdown, JSON or CSV at any time.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Memos itself carries no license fee: the software is entirely free and open source under the MIT license, with no paid tiers, subscriptions or per-user charges built into the product.

The only cost users incur is whatever infrastructure they choose to run it on, such as a small VPS, a home server, or a container hosting platform, which for a lightweight single-Go-binary app is typically inexpensive.

features.title: Markdown-Native Editing
features.description: Write notes in Markdown with syntax highlighting, tables and LaTeX math support built directly into the editor.

features.title: Self-Hosted Deployment
features.description: Runs as a single Go binary or ~20MB Docker image on a Raspberry Pi, VPS or cloud server, with SQLite, MySQL or PostgreSQL as the data store.

features.title: Full-Text Search and Tagging
features.description: A flexible tag system combined with full-text search makes it fast to organize and retrieve past notes.

features.title: Media Attachments
features.description: Drag-and-drop support for images, video, audio and documents directly into any memo.

features.title: Selective Public Sharing
features.description: Share individual memos publicly via a simple link while keeping the rest of the notebook private.

features.title: Data Portability
features.description: Export all notes to Markdown, JSON or CSV at any time, avoiding lock-in to a proprietary format.

features.title: REST and gRPC APIs
features.description: Open APIs allow developers to build custom clients, browser extensions and third-party integrations.

features.title: Zero Telemetry
features.description: No usage tracking or analytics are sent to any third party, since the entire application runs on infrastructure the user controls.

pros: Completely free and open source under the MIT license
Full data ownership through self-hosting, with no vendor lock-in
Lightweight and fast, with a small resource footprint suitable for a Raspberry Pi
Simple, quick-capture timeline interface that is faster than heavier note apps for short-form notes
Active open-source community contributing translations, themes and integrations
cons: Requires technical comfort with Docker or server administration to self-host
No official managed cloud hosting, so users are responsible for backups and uptime
Timeline-based structure is less suited to long-form documents or deeply nested wikis
Fewer built-in collaboration features than commercial team note tools
No official enterprise support contract or SLA since it is a community project

faqs.question: Is Memos free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, Memos is completely free and open source under the MIT license, with no subscription fees or paid tiers.

faqs.question: Do I need to self-host Memos?
faqs.answer: Yes, Memos does not offer an official managed cloud version, so users deploy it themselves on a server, VPS, home device such as a Raspberry Pi, or a container platform such as Docker.

faqs.question: What databases does Memos support?
faqs.answer: Memos supports SQLite, MySQL and PostgreSQL as its storage backend, letting users choose based on the scale of their deployment.

faqs.question: Can I export my notes out of Memos?
faqs.answer: Yes, Memos supports exporting notes to Markdown, JSON or CSV, so data always remains portable.

faqs.question: Does Memos support Markdown and attachments?
faqs.answer: Yes, Memos is Markdown-native and supports syntax highlighting, tables, LaTeX math, and drag-and-drop images, video, audio and document attachments.

faqs.question: Who maintains Memos?
faqs.answer: Memos is maintained by the usememos open-source community on GitHub, led by its original developer, with contributions from a large community of volunteers.

use_cases.title: Personal Daily Journaling
use_cases.description: Individuals use Memos as a quick-capture timeline for daily thoughts, to-dos and links, similar to a personal microblog.

use_cases.title: Self-Hosted Team Notes
use_cases.description: Small technical teams deploy a shared Memos instance on internal infrastructure to jot down quick updates and shared references without adopting a paid SaaS tool.

use_cases.title: Home Lab and Raspberry Pi Projects
use_cases.description: Hobbyists running home labs use Memos as a lightweight self-hosted app alongside other services, since it requires minimal resources.

enrichment.short_description: Mend.io is an enterprise application security platform that combines software composition analysis, static analysis and automated remediation to secure open-source and custom code.
enrichment.starting_price: Custom pricing (contact sales); per-developer plans have been reported from roughly $250 to $1,000 per contributing developer per year
enrichment.headquarters: Tel Aviv-Yafo, Israel (with a US office in Boston, Massachusetts)
enrichment.seo_title: Mend.io Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Mend.io (formerly WhiteSource) review: software composition analysis, SAST and automated remediation for application security. Features, pricing, alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Mend.io is an application security platform focused on finding and automatically remediating vulnerabilities in open-source components and custom application code. The company was founded in 2011 in Israel as WhiteSource Software by Rami Sass, Azi Cohen and Ron Rymon, with a mission to automate the discovery, tracking and remediation of open-source software risk before the software composition analysis (SCA) category had an established name. WhiteSource grew from a small Israeli startup into a global vendor serving hundreds of enterprise customers, and in May 2022 the company rebranded from WhiteSource to Mend.io to reflect an expanded focus beyond inventory and license compliance toward automated detection, remediation and prevention of application security issues. As part of that expansion the company acquired the software supply-chain security startup Diffend in April 2021, and the static-analysis vendors Xanitizer and DefenseCode in February 2022, folding SAST capability directly into its platform. Mend.io is headquartered in Tel Aviv-Yafo, Israel, with a significant North American presence out of Boston, Massachusetts, and employs roughly 250 to 290 people globally as of 2026. The core Mend platform is delivered as a cloud-based SaaS service that integrates into a customer's software development lifecycle: it scans source code repositories, package manifests and build artifacts to build a real-time inventory of open-source dependencies, flags known vulnerabilities (CVEs) and license-compliance issues, and can automatically generate remediation pull requests that bump a vulnerable dependency to a patched version. Its product suite includes Mend SCA for software composition analysis, Mend SAST for static application security testing of proprietary code, Mend Renovate Enterprise for automated dependency updates (built on the popular open-source Renovate bot, which Mend also stewards), and Mend AI, which extends inventory and risk scanning to AI and machine-learning components and models used inside an application. Pricing is not published transparently on the company's website and is typically quoted per 'contributing developer' per year, with third-party sources citing tiers in roughly the $250 (Renovate Enterprise) to $1,000 (full Mend AppSec bundle) per-developer range, alongside a free trial available on request; enterprise contracts are negotiated directly with sales. Mend.io's target customers are mid-market to large enterprises with mature DevSecOps practices that need to satisfy compliance requirements around open-source usage. Its main competitors include Snyk, GitHub Advanced Security, Sonatype, Checkmarx, Veracode and Black Duck (Synopsys). Mend.io differentiates itself primarily through automated, reachability-aware remediation, and through its stewardship of the widely used open-source Renovate dependency-update tool, which gives it deep integration into existing developer workflows across GitHub, GitLab and Bitbucket.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Mend.io is an application security company that helps organizations find, prioritize and automatically fix vulnerabilities in the open-source components and custom code that make up modern software. Formerly known as WhiteSource, the company rebranded in 2022 to reflect a broader platform that goes beyond inventory and license tracking into automated remediation.

The platform is aimed at engineering and security teams inside mid-market and enterprise organizations that need to manage open-source risk at scale, satisfy compliance frameworks, and reduce the manual burden of patching vulnerable dependencies across large codebases.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Mend's core capability is software composition analysis (SCA): it scans a codebase's package manifests and lockfiles to build a real-time bill of materials, matches dependencies against known vulnerability databases, and flags license-compliance risks. Automated remediation pull requests can bump a flagged dependency directly to a patched version.

Beyond SCA, Mend offers static application security testing (SAST) for first-party code, container and cloud-native scanning, and Mend AI for inventorying and assessing risk in AI and machine-learning components. The company also stewards Renovate, a widely used open-source dependency-update bot, and offers an enterprise version with additional controls.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Mend.io does not publish transparent list pricing; costs are quoted per 'contributing developer' per year and negotiated through the sales team, with free trials or proof-of-concept access available on request.

Third-party sources report tiers ranging from roughly $250 per developer per year for Mend Renovate Enterprise up to around $1,000 per developer per year for the full Mend AppSec bundle that combines SCA, SAST, Renovate and AI component inventory.

features.title: Software Composition Analysis
features.description: Builds a real-time inventory of open-source dependencies and flags known CVEs and license-compliance risks.

features.title: Automated Remediation
features.description: Generates pull requests that automatically update vulnerable dependencies to patched versions rather than only reporting them.

features.title: Static Application Security Testing
features.description: Scans proprietary, first-party code for security vulnerabilities alongside open-source dependency risk.

features.title: Renovate Dependency Automation
features.description: Mend stewards the popular open-source Renovate bot, offering an enterprise edition for automated, policy-driven dependency updates.

features.title: Mend AI
features.description: Extends inventory and risk scanning to AI models and machine-learning components used inside applications.

features.title: Reachability Analysis
features.description: Prioritizes vulnerabilities based on whether the vulnerable code path is actually reachable and exploitable, reducing alert noise.

features.title: License Compliance Tracking
features.description: Flags open-source license risks alongside security vulnerabilities to support legal and compliance review.

features.title: CI/CD and SCM Integrations
features.description: Integrates with GitHub, GitLab, Bitbucket and major CI/CD pipelines to scan code as part of the existing development workflow.

pros: Strong automated remediation that generates fix pull requests rather than just alerts
Broad platform covering SCA, SAST, container and AI component security in one product
Deep dependency-update integration through its stewardship of the open-source Renovate bot
Established vendor with over a decade of experience in the software composition analysis category
Reachability-based prioritization helps reduce false-positive alert fatigue
cons: Pricing is not published and requires a sales conversation for every deal
Per-developer pricing can become expensive for large engineering organizations
Some users report a learning curve configuring policies across a large monorepo estate
Primarily targeted at enterprise buyers, with less self-serve accessibility for small teams
Overlapping product lines from acquisitions can add platform complexity

faqs.question: What was Mend.io previously called?
faqs.answer: Mend.io was known as WhiteSource Software until it rebranded in May 2022 to reflect its expanded focus on automated remediation.

faqs.question: What does Mend.io do?
faqs.answer: Mend.io provides software composition analysis, static application security testing and automated remediation to help organizations secure open-source and custom application code.

faqs.question: How is Mend.io priced?
faqs.answer: Pricing is quoted per contributing developer per year and is not published publicly; buyers need to contact sales for a quote, though free trials are available.

faqs.question: Where is Mend.io headquartered?
faqs.answer: Mend.io is headquartered in Tel Aviv-Yafo, Israel, with a significant office presence in Boston, Massachusetts.

faqs.question: Does Mend.io offer a free trial?
faqs.answer: Yes, Mend.io offers free trials and proof-of-concept access on request, though it does not have a permanent free tier.

faqs.question: What is Mend Renovate?
faqs.answer: Mend Renovate is an automated dependency-update tool, built on the open-source Renovate bot that Mend stewards, available in a free open-source form and a paid Enterprise edition.

use_cases.title: Enterprise Open-Source Risk Management
use_cases.description: Large engineering organizations use Mend to maintain a real-time inventory of open-source dependencies and automatically remediate known vulnerabilities across hundreds of repositories.
use_cases.audience: Enterprise security and platform engineering teams

use_cases.title: Regulatory and License Compliance
use_cases.description: Legal and compliance teams use Mend's license-tracking features to ensure open-source usage complies with company policy and software supply-chain regulations.
use_cases.audience: Compliance and legal teams at software companies

use_cases.title: Automated Dependency Updates
use_cases.description: Development teams use Mend Renovate to automatically open pull requests that keep dependencies patched and up to date without manual tracking.

enrichment.short_description: Mention is a real-time social listening and media monitoring platform that tracks brand, competitor and industry mentions across social media, news, blogs, forums and review sites.
enrichment.seo_title: Mention Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Mention review: real-time social listening and media monitoring across social, news and review sites. See features, pricing plans and top alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Mention is a social listening and media monitoring platform that lets brands, agencies and individuals track mentions of their name, products, competitors or any keyword across social media, news sites, blogs, forums, videos, images and review platforms in real time. The product originated in November 2011 inside eFounders (now Hexa), a Paris-based startup studio, where developer Arnaud Le Blanc wrote the first version alongside Edouard de la Joncquiere; the two officially co-founded the company in 2013, and Mention is generally described as having launched commercially in 2012. Matthieu Vaxelaire, previously a partner at eFounders, became Mention's CEO in October 2014 and led the company through a period of rapid growth, reportedly reaching hundreds of thousands of users within a few years of launch. Mention remains headquartered in Paris, France, and has grown to serve customers across more than 125 countries. Architecturally, Mention operates as a cloud-based SaaS platform that continuously crawls and indexes web and social content, using Boolean search logic (AND, OR, NOT, wildcards) to let users build precise monitoring queries called 'alerts.' It covers major social networks including Facebook, Instagram, X (formerly Twitter), Reddit, TikTok, Pinterest and YouTube, general web sources such as news outlets, blogs and forums, and more than 75 review sites including Google, Trustpilot, Amazon and Glassdoor. On top of raw monitoring, Mention layers analytics such as sentiment analysis, share-of-voice and competitor benchmarking, influence scoring to identify high-impact authors, and customizable reporting dashboards that can be white-labeled for agency use. Pricing is subscription-based with tiers historically named Solo, Pro, ProPlus and Company/Enterprise; the entry Solo plan has been priced around $49 per month with one to two alerts and a single user, scaling up through higher-volume plans with more users, alerts and add-ons such as historical data access, Slack and Zapier integrations, and API access, up to custom Enterprise pricing with dedicated account management. A 14-day free trial is typically available, though Mention has at various points discontinued a permanent free tier. Mention's target audience spans marketing and communications teams at small and mid-sized businesses, PR and social media agencies managing multiple client brands, and enterprise brand-management teams needing to track reputation and campaign performance across many markets and languages. Its principal competitors include Brandwatch, Sprout Social, Meltwater, Talkwalker, Awario, Brand24 and Mentionlytics. Mention differentiates itself with an accessible, mid-market price point relative to enterprise-grade tools like Brandwatch or Meltwater, combined with broad source coverage and an agency-friendly white-label reporting feature, positioning it between lightweight free mention trackers and expensive enterprise social intelligence suites.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Mention is a media monitoring and social listening tool that helps businesses, agencies and individuals keep track of what is being said about their brand, products, competitors or any topic of interest across the web. It was created in Paris in 2011-2012 inside the eFounders startup studio and has since grown into a platform used in over 125 countries.

The platform is built around 'alerts', which are saved Boolean search queries that continuously scan social networks, news sites, blogs, forums and review platforms for matching mentions, surfacing results in a real-time dashboard along with sentiment and volume analytics.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Mention monitors major social networks such as Facebook, Instagram, X, Reddit, TikTok, Pinterest and YouTube, general web content like news articles, blogs and forums, and more than 75 review sites including Google, Trustpilot, Amazon and Glassdoor. Boolean search operators let users build precise alerts to filter out noise.

On top of raw monitoring, Mention provides sentiment analysis, share-of-voice tracking, competitor benchmarking, influencer and author scoring, and customizable, white-labelable reporting dashboards, which makes it popular with PR and social media agencies managing multiple client brands.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Mention is sold on a subscription basis with tiers that scale by number of users, alerts and add-on features. The entry-level Solo plan is priced at roughly $49 per month for a single user and a small number of alerts, with lower effective pricing available on annual billing.

Higher tiers add more users, alerts, historical data access, integrations such as Slack and Zapier, and API access, while the top Company or Enterprise tier is custom-priced and includes a dedicated account manager; a 14-day free trial is generally available to test the platform.

features.title: Real-Time Social and Web Monitoring
features.description: Continuously tracks mentions across major social networks, news sites, blogs and forums as they happen.

features.title: Review Site Monitoring
features.description: Tracks mentions and ratings across more than 75 review platforms including Google, Trustpilot, Amazon and Glassdoor.

features.title: Boolean Search Alerts
features.description: Lets users build precise monitoring queries using AND, OR, NOT and wildcard operators to reduce irrelevant results.

features.title: Sentiment Analysis
features.description: Automatically classifies mentions as positive, negative or neutral to gauge brand perception over time.

features.title: Competitor Benchmarking
features.description: Compares share of voice and mention volume against named competitors.

features.title: Influence Scoring
features.description: Ranks authors and sources by estimated reach and influence to help prioritize outreach.

features.title: White-Label Reporting
features.description: Agencies can generate and export custom-branded reports for clients.

features.title: Team Collaboration Tools
features.description: Multi-user access controls let marketing and PR teams share alerts, dashboards and reports.

pros: Broad source coverage spanning social media, news, forums and dedicated review sites
Boolean search gives precise control over what counts as a relevant mention
Agency-friendly white-label reporting and multi-client management
Mid-market pricing that is more accessible than enterprise suites like Brandwatch or Meltwater
Established track record, operating since the early 2010s with broad international coverage
cons: No permanent free tier, only a time-limited free trial
Entry-level Solo plan is limited to a single user and very few alerts
Some integrations, such as Slack and Zapier, are gated behind paid add-ons
Less deep historical and archival data access compared to enterprise-grade competitors
Sentiment analysis accuracy, like most automated tools, can struggle with sarcasm or industry-specific language

faqs.question: What does Mention do?
faqs.answer: Mention is a social listening and media monitoring platform that tracks brand, competitor and keyword mentions across social media, news, blogs, forums and review sites in real time.

faqs.question: How much does Mention cost?
faqs.answer: Mention's entry-level Solo plan starts at around $49 per month, with Pro, ProPlus and custom Enterprise/Company tiers available as needs scale up.

faqs.question: Does Mention have a free trial?
faqs.answer: Yes, Mention offers a 14-day free trial, though it does not currently offer a permanent free plan.

faqs.question: Where is Mention based?
faqs.answer: Mention is headquartered in Paris, France, and was originally developed inside the eFounders startup studio.

faqs.question: What sources does Mention monitor?
faqs.answer: Mention monitors social networks such as Facebook, Instagram, X, TikTok, Reddit, Pinterest and YouTube, plus news sites, blogs, forums, and more than 75 review platforms.

faqs.question: Is Mention suitable for agencies?
faqs.answer: Yes, Mention offers white-label reporting and multi-client management features that are commonly used by PR and social media agencies.

use_cases.title: Brand Reputation Monitoring
use_cases.description: Marketing and communications teams track what customers and press are saying about their brand across social and news channels in real time.

use_cases.title: Agency Client Reporting
use_cases.description: PR and social media agencies use Mention to monitor multiple client brands and generate white-labeled reports.

use_cases.title: Competitive Intelligence
use_cases.description: Companies track competitor mentions and share of voice to benchmark campaign performance and market positioning.

enrichment.short_description: Merchynt is an AI-powered local SEO platform that automates Google Business Profile optimization, citation building and Google Maps ranking for small businesses and agencies.
enrichment.starting_price: $99/month per Google Business Profile (Paige AI plan)
enrichment.seo_title: Merchynt Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Merchynt review: AI-powered local SEO and Google Business Profile tool. See Paige AI features, pricing plans, agency program and top alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Merchynt is a local SEO software company that builds AI-powered tools to help small businesses and marketing agencies optimize and manage their Google Business Profile (formerly Google My Business) listings and improve rankings in Google Maps and local search results. The company was founded by Justin Silverman around 2019 and is headquartered in Santa Monica, California. Merchynt's flagship product is Paige, an AI agent positioned as an autonomous local SEO assistant that continuously analyzes a business's Google Business Profile, competitor listings and local ranking factors, then automatically makes optimizations such as updating business descriptions, adding relevant posts, managing Q&A, monitoring reviews, and adjusting categories and attributes to improve visibility. Paige is priced at $99 per month per Google Business Profile, with a low-cost trial offer (as little as $1) available so prospective customers can see initial results before committing to the full subscription. Alongside Paige, Merchynt offers a Citation Software product that automates the creation and syncing of business citations across more than 60 online directories, priced at $40 per month per profile for regular customers, dropping to $20 per month for members of Merchynt's Agency Partner Program. For businesses that prefer a done-for-you approach rather than self-service software, Merchynt also sells professional Google Business Profile management services, with monthly retainers reported in the range of $125 to $400 plus one-time setup or profile-overhaul fees around $499. Merchynt operates a dedicated Agency Partner Program, priced at roughly $100 per month, that bundles discounted access to Paige and Citation Software, a lead-generation tool called Sam, sales and marketing materials, priority support and access to a community of local SEO agency owners. Sam itself is offered as a free-forever lead tool with a pay-as-you-go option for additional leads. Merchynt's target customers are local and multi-location small businesses (restaurants, home services, medical practices, retail) that want to rank higher on Google Maps without hiring an in-house SEO specialist, as well as marketing and SEO agencies managing local SEO for many client locations who want to white-label and scale their services using AI automation. Its main competitors in the local SEO and Google Business Profile management space include SOCi, Yext, BrightLocal, Whitespark, Synup and SE Ranking's local tools. Merchynt differentiates itself through its AI-agent approach, its low-friction trial pricing, and its strong focus on serving agencies through a dedicated partner program and white-label options rather than only targeting end businesses directly.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Merchynt is a local SEO software company focused on helping small businesses and the agencies that serve them rank higher in Google Maps and local search results. Its main product, an AI agent called Paige, is designed to autonomously manage and optimize a business's Google Business Profile on an ongoing basis.

Founded around 2019 and based in Santa Monica, California, Merchynt has grown a product suite that spans AI-driven profile optimization, automated citation building, and professional local SEO management services, alongside a dedicated program for agencies that manage local SEO on behalf of clients.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Paige, Merchynt's flagship AI tool, continuously monitors a Google Business Profile and competitor listings, then automatically publishes posts, manages business information, tracks review trends and adjusts profile attributes to improve local ranking, aiming to replace much of the manual work of a local SEO specialist.

Merchynt's Citation Software automatically creates and keeps business citations synced across more than 60 online directories, while its Sam tool helps agencies generate new client leads. Together, these tools plus done-for-you management services give Merchynt both a self-service software offering and a managed-service option.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Paige is priced at $99 per month per Google Business Profile, with a low-cost trial (as little as $1) available to preview results before paying full price. Citation Software costs an additional $40 per month per profile.

Agencies can join the Agency Partner Program for around $100 per month to get discounted rates on Paige and Citation Software, free access to the Sam lead-generation tool, and priority support, while businesses wanting fully managed services can instead purchase professional management packages typically priced from $125 to $400 per month.

features.title: Paige AI Local SEO Agent
features.description: An autonomous AI agent that continuously optimizes a Google Business Profile, including posts, Q&A, attributes and categories.

features.title: Automated Citation Building
features.description: Creates and syncs business citations across more than 60 online directories with minimal manual setup.

features.title: Review Management
features.description: Monitors and helps manage Google reviews as part of ongoing profile optimization.

features.title: White-Label Agency Tools
features.description: Agencies can white-label Paige and reporting for their own clients under the Agency Partner Program.

features.title: Sam Lead Generation
features.description: A free lead-generation tool that surfaces local businesses that may need SEO help, aimed at agencies.

features.title: Professional Management Services
features.description: Done-for-you Google Business Profile setup and ongoing management for businesses that prefer a managed service.

features.title: Multi-Location Support
features.description: Per-profile pricing structure supports businesses and agencies managing many locations or clients.

pros: Automates much of the day-to-day work of Google Business Profile optimization
Low-cost trial option lets businesses see results before committing to a full subscription
Dedicated agency program with discounted rates and white-label options
Covers both self-service software and fully managed service tiers
Focused specifically on local and Google Maps SEO rather than broad general SEO
cons: Per-profile pricing can add up quickly for businesses or agencies managing many locations
Citation building and other add-ons carry additional monthly costs on top of the base Paige plan
As an AI-automated tool, results depend heavily on Google's frequently changing local ranking algorithm
Less brand recognition than larger local SEO platforms such as Yext or SOCi
Managed-service tier pricing requires a custom quote rather than transparent self-checkout

faqs.question: What does Merchynt do?
faqs.answer: Merchynt provides AI-powered local SEO tools, most notably an AI agent called Paige, that optimize and manage Google Business Profile listings to improve Google Maps rankings.

faqs.question: How much does Merchynt cost?
faqs.answer: Merchynt's Paige AI plan is priced at $99 per month per Google Business Profile, with additional add-ons such as Citation Software priced separately.

faqs.question: Does Merchynt offer a free trial?
faqs.answer: Yes, Merchynt offers a low-cost trial, sometimes as low as $1, so businesses can evaluate results before paying the full monthly price.

faqs.question: Is Merchynt built for agencies?
faqs.answer: Yes, Merchynt runs a dedicated Agency Partner Program offering discounted pricing, white-label tools and a lead-generation tool called Sam for agencies managing multiple client locations.

faqs.question: Where is Merchynt based?
faqs.answer: Merchynt is headquartered in Santa Monica, California.

faqs.question: What is Paige?

enrichment.short_description: Mercury is a fintech platform that provides business banking, including checking, savings, credit cards and treasury tools, built primarily for startups and scaling companies.
enrichment.seo_title: Mercury Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Mercury review: startup-focused business banking with checking, savings, credit cards and treasury tools. See features, pricing plans and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Mercury is a financial technology company that provides banking services designed specifically for startups and scaling businesses. It was founded in 2017 in San Francisco by Immad Akhund, Max Tagher and Jason Zhang; Akhund, the CEO, had previously sold his mobile advertising startup for $45 million in 2016, while Tagher and Zhang had worked with him previously. Mercury is a fintech, not a chartered bank itself: banking services are provided through partner banks, with deposits eligible for FDIC insurance through those partner banks and sweep networks. The company has raised several hundred million dollars in primary and secondary funding across multiple rounds from investors including Sequoia Capital, Andreessen Horowitz, Coatue, CRV and Spark Capital, reaching a reported valuation of about $5.2 billion as of a 2026 funding round. Mercury's core product is a free business checking and savings account with no monthly fees, accessed through a web and mobile banking dashboard, and includes features such as bill pay, basic invoicing, expense reimbursements, and free accounting integrations with QuickBooks and Xero. Beyond the free tier, Mercury offers two paid plans, Mercury Plus at roughly $29.90 per month and Mercury Pro at roughly $299 per month (with discounts for annual billing), which unlock additional invoicing volume, more reimbursement seats, a dedicated relationship manager, NetSuite integration and unlimited 1099 tax filing support. Mercury has also expanded into corporate credit cards, venture debt facilitation, treasury and money-market fund access for idle cash, and AI-assisted tools for founders. The target audience is primarily venture-backed and bootstrapped technology startups, along with e-commerce and other digitally native small businesses that want a modern, API-friendly banking experience without the friction of traditional business banking at incumbent banks. Mercury's main competitors include Brex, Ramp, Rho, Novo, Relay and the business-banking divisions of traditional banks. Mercury differentiates itself through a startup-native product experience, deep integrations with startup tooling, and a design-forward interface, while positioning itself as a comprehensive finance stack rather than a single banking product.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Mercury is a financial technology company offering business banking built specifically for startups and growing companies. Founded in San Francisco in 2017 by Immad Akhund, Max Tagher and Jason Zhang, Mercury pairs a modern web and mobile banking interface with the account infrastructure of partner banks, since Mercury itself is not a chartered bank.

The platform has grown beyond checking and savings into a broader finance stack for startups, including corporate credit cards, treasury and money-market access for idle cash, venture debt facilitation, and accounting integrations, positioning itself as an alternative to traditional business banking at incumbent institutions.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Mercury's free plan includes business checking and savings accounts, unlimited bill pay, basic invoicing, expense reimbursements for a handful of active users, and free accounting automations for QuickBooks and Xero, all with no monthly account fees.

Paid Plus and Pro tiers add features such as recurring and API-driven invoicing at higher volumes, more reimbursement seats, a dedicated relationship manager on the Pro tier, NetSuite categorization support, and unlimited 1099 tax filings, aimed at companies with more complex finance operations.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Mercury's base account is free, with business checking, savings, debit and credit card transactions and core banking features included at no monthly cost.

Two paid tiers extend the platform: Mercury Plus at roughly $29.90 per month (about $23.95 per month billed annually) and Mercury Pro at roughly $299 per month (about $239.90 billed annually), each unlocking higher invoicing volumes, more reimbursement seats and additional integrations.

features.description: Manage employee expense reimbursements for a set number of active users per month, scaling with the paid plan.

features.title: Treasury and Idle Cash Management
features.description: Access to money-market funds and treasury tools to earn yield on excess operating cash.

features.title: Accounting Integrations
features.description: Free, automated syncing with QuickBooks and Xero to streamline bookkeeping.

features.title: 1099 Tax Filing
features.description: Basic 1099 filing included on the free plan, with unlimited filings on paid tiers.

pros: Free core banking account with no monthly fees or minimum balance requirements
Modern, developer- and founder-friendly interface with strong startup ecosystem integrations
Backed by well-known venture investors and has raised substantial funding, suggesting financial stability
Bundles banking, invoicing, treasury and credit into a single platform
Popular and well-reviewed among the venture-backed startup community
cons: Not a chartered bank itself, so accounts rely on partner-bank infrastructure that has changed over time
Advanced features such as a dedicated relationship manager require the higher-cost Pro plan
Primarily optimized for US-based startups, with more limited international support than some competitors
Fee-free ACH debit invoicing requires the Pro plan; Plus users pay per transaction
As with any fintech relying on partner banks, account changes tied to banking-partner transitions have occurred historically

faqs.question: Is Mercury a bank?
faqs.answer: No, Mercury is a financial technology company, not a chartered bank; banking services are provided through partner banks, with deposits eligible for FDIC insurance through those partners.

faqs.question: How much does Mercury cost?
faqs.answer: Mercury's core account is free, with optional Plus (about $29.90/month) and Pro (about $299/month) plans that add higher invoicing volumes and additional features.

faqs.question: Who founded Mercury?
faqs.answer: Mercury was founded in 2017 by Immad Akhund, Max Tagher and Jason Zhang in San Francisco.

faqs.question: Who is Mercury designed for?
faqs.answer: Mercury is designed primarily for startups and scaling technology companies, though it also serves e-commerce and other digitally native small businesses.

faqs.question: Does Mercury offer credit cards?
faqs.answer: Yes, Mercury offers corporate credit cards integrated into its banking dashboard alongside checking, savings and treasury products.

faqs.question: How much funding has Mercury raised?
faqs.answer: Mercury has raised several hundred million dollars in funding from investors including Sequoia Capital, Andreessen Horowitz and Coatue, and was valued at approximately $5.2 billion in a 2026 round.

enrichment.short_description: Mere Medical is an open-source, offline-first personal health record app that aggregates and syncs medical records from multiple patient portals using FHIR standards.
enrichment.seo_title: Mere Medical Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Mere Medical review: an open-source, self-hosted personal health record app that aggregates records from patient portals via FHIR. Features and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Mere Medical is an open-source, self-hostable personal health record (PHR) application built to aggregate a person's medical records from multiple patient portals into a single, unified timeline. The project is created and maintained by developer Chris Fu (GitHub handle cfu288) and is distributed under the MIT license, with its source code publicly available on GitHub. Rather than requiring users to log into each hospital's or clinic's separate patient portal individually, Mere connects to healthcare providers' systems using HL7 FHIR (Fast Healthcare Interoperability Resources) patient-access APIs, authenticated through SMART-on-FHIR, the widely adopted standard that many US hospital systems support as a result of federal interoperability regulations. Once connected, Mere pulls in clinical data such as lab results, immunizations, medications, clinical notes and visit summaries, and normalizes them into a single, searchable, chronological health timeline that spans every connected provider. Architecturally, the application is described as offline-first and local-first: records are stored directly on the user's own device rather than a centralized cloud server, and the project supports client-side, zero-knowledge encryption so that even self-hosted deployments keep sensitive health data under the user's exclusive control. Mere is listed in the SMART Health IT App Gallery, the official directory of apps built on the SMART-on-FHIR standard, and the project has been presented in an academic context at the American Medical Informatics Association (AMIA) symposium as an example of an open-source approach to patient-centered health record aggregation. Core features include a unified health timeline across all connected providers, consolidated summary views such as an aggregated immunization history, cross-provider document and lab search, laboratory value trending over time to visualize how specific test results change across multiple visits and providers, and access to clinical and provider notes where available through the FHIR API. Because it is open source and self-hosted (or run locally as a personal web app), Mere Medical carries no license fee and no company operates it as a commercial SaaS product; there is no vendor lock-in, subscription, or centralized data broker involved. The target users are privacy-conscious patients managing chronic conditions across multiple health systems, caregivers coordinating care for family members seen by different providers, and developers or researchers interested in patient-facing FHIR tooling. Its closest comparisons are other personal health record and patient-data-aggregation tools such as Apple Health Records and CommonHealth, but Mere differentiates itself by being fully open source, self-hostable, and architected around local-first, zero-knowledge-encrypted storage rather than a centralized company-run cloud service, giving users complete control over where their medical data lives.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Mere Medical is an open-source personal health record application that lets individuals pull together medical records scattered across multiple hospital and clinic patient portals into one unified, searchable timeline. It is built and maintained by an independent developer and distributed freely under the MIT license.

The app connects to healthcare providers using the HL7 FHIR patient-access standard with SMART-on-FHIR authentication, the same interoperability standard many US health systems support due to federal patient-data-access regulations, meaning users do not need proprietary integrations from each hospital to consolidate their records.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Once connected to a patient portal, Mere pulls in labs, immunizations, medications, clinical notes and visit summaries, then presents them as a single chronological timeline that spans every connected provider, along with consolidated views like a full immunization history and lab-value trending across visits.

The application is offline-first and local-first, storing records directly on the user's device rather than a centralized server, and supports zero-knowledge client-side encryption, giving users full control over their sensitive health data even in self-hosted deployments.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Mere Medical is free and open source under the MIT license, with the source code publicly available on GitHub and no company operating it as a paid SaaS product.

Because the app can be run locally or self-hosted, the only potential cost is whatever infrastructure a user chooses for self-hosting; there are no subscription fees, premium tiers, or per-record charges built into the software itself.

features.title: Unified Health Timeline
features.description: Aggregates records from multiple connected patient portals into a single chronological view.

features.title: FHIR and SMART-on-FHIR Connectivity
features.description: Connects to healthcare providers using the standard HL7 FHIR patient-access API and SMART-on-FHIR authentication.

features.title: Lab Value Trending
features.description: Visualizes how specific lab results change over time across multiple visits and providers.

features.title: Local-First, Offline Storage
features.description: Stores health records directly on the user's device rather than a centralized cloud server.

features.title: Zero-Knowledge Encryption
features.description: Supports client-side encryption so sensitive medical data remains under the user's exclusive control.

features.title: Cross-Provider Document Search
features.description: Lets users search across documents, notes and results from every connected provider at once.

pros: Free and fully open source under the MIT license
Aggregates records from multiple health systems without needing separate logins for each portal
Local-first architecture keeps sensitive medical data on the user's own device
Built on the standard FHIR/SMART-on-FHIR interoperability protocol rather than proprietary integrations
Listed in the official SMART Health IT app gallery, indicating standards compliance
cons: Requires providers to support FHIR/SMART-on-FHIR patient-access APIs, which not every clinic offers
As a small, independently maintained open-source project, it lacks dedicated commercial support or an SLA
Self-hosting or local setup requires more technical comfort than a typical consumer health app
Feature set is more limited than large commercial patient-portal aggregators backed by major tech companies
Depends on continued volunteer maintenance since there is no company behind the project

faqs.question: Is Mere Medical free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Mere Medical is free and open source under the MIT license, with no subscription fees.

faqs.question: How does Mere Medical connect to my medical records?
faqs.answer: It uses the HL7 FHIR patient-access API with SMART-on-FHIR authentication, the interoperability standard supported by many US healthcare providers, to pull records directly from patient portals.

faqs.question: Where is my health data stored with Mere Medical?
faqs.answer: Mere Medical is local-first and offline-first, storing records directly on the user's own device rather than a centralized cloud server, with support for zero-knowledge encryption.

faqs.question: Who maintains Mere Medical?
faqs.answer: Mere Medical is an independent open-source project created and maintained by developer Chris Fu, with the source code publicly available on GitHub.

faqs.question: Can Mere Medical connect to any hospital?
faqs.answer: Only providers that support the FHIR patient-access standard with SMART-on-FHIR authentication can be connected, so coverage depends on each provider's own systems.

faqs.question: Is Mere Medical the same as Apple Health Records?
faqs.answer: No, Mere Medical is an independent open-source project rather than a feature of a commercial operating system, though it serves a similar purpose of aggregating records from multiple providers using FHIR.

use_cases.title: Chronic Condition Record Aggregation
use_cases.description: Patients managing a chronic condition across several specialists use Mere to see all their labs and notes in one timeline.
use_cases.audience: Patients with complex or chronic health conditions

use_cases.title: Family Caregiving Coordination
use_cases.description: Caregivers use Mere to consolidate records for a family member seen across multiple providers and health systems.

use_cases.title: Self-Hosted Personal Health Archive
use_cases.description: Privacy-focused individuals self-host Mere to maintain a personal, offline-first archive of their full medical history.

enrichment.short_description: Mesh Payments is a corporate card and spend management platform offering cardless payments, automated expense management and travel and expense controls for businesses.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (Pro plan for up to 3 users); Premium from $13/user/month
enrichment.seo_title: Mesh Payments Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Mesh Payments review: corporate cards and spend management with cardless payments and automated expense controls. Features, pricing and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Mesh Payments is a corporate spend management platform that provides businesses with virtual and physical corporate cards, automated expense management, and travel and expense controls, aiming to give finance teams full visibility into company spend. The company was founded in 2018 by Eran Katoni and Oded Zehavi and is headquartered in New York, with additional operations in Israel reflecting its founders' background. Mesh has raised approximately $123 million in total funding from investors including Tiger Global Management, TLV Partners and Alpha Wave Global, with a Series C round of $60 million closing in September 2022 following a $50 million raise in December 2021; the company has also pursued debt financing to support growth. As of its most recent public figures, Mesh reports serving more than 1,000 customers with close to $1 billion in annualized total payment volume flowing through the platform. Functionally, Mesh issues cardless and physical corporate cards that can be provisioned instantly and governed by granular, rule-based spend controls (per-vendor, per-category or per-employee limits), automatically reconciles transactions with receipts and accounting codes to reduce manual expense-report work, and provides real-time dashboards giving finance leaders visibility into spend as it happens rather than after the fact through end-of-month statements. The platform also includes dedicated travel and expense (T&E) management functionality, letting companies manage travel bookings and related spend within the same system as everyday card spend, and offers cashback on card transactions. Mesh's pricing model is notably freemium for a corporate card platform: its base Pro tier is free for companies with up to three users, offering unlimited cards, no long-term commitment, and cashback rewards, while a Premium tier is priced at roughly $13 per user per month for more advanced spend-control and reporting features, and an Enterprise tier offers custom pricing for large organizations with complex, global spend and travel needs. The target customer base spans venture-backed startups and mid-market companies, particularly SaaS and technology businesses, that need centralized control over distributed employee and vendor spend without the overhead of traditional expense-report processes. Mesh's primary competitors include Ramp, Brex, Airbase, Bill.com's Divvy, Emburse and Coupa. Mesh differentiates itself through its cardless-first approach to instant card issuance, its combined spend-and-travel management in one platform, and a freemium pricing structure that lowers the barrier to entry for smaller teams compared to some competitors that require minimum spend commitments or charge for every user from day one.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Mesh Payments is a corporate spend management platform that gives businesses virtual and physical corporate cards alongside automated expense management and travel and expense (T&E) controls. It was founded in 2018 by Eran Katoni and Oded Zehavi and is headquartered in New York.

The platform is designed to give finance teams real-time visibility into company spend as it happens, replacing the reconciliation-after-the-fact model of traditional corporate cards and manual expense reports with rule-based controls and automated categorization.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Mesh issues cardless and physical corporate cards that can be provisioned instantly and governed by granular spend rules set per vendor, category or employee, giving finance teams control over spend before it happens rather than after.

The platform automatically reconciles transactions with receipts and accounting codes, reducing manual expense-report work, and includes dedicated travel and expense management functionality alongside real-time dashboards and cashback on card spend.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Mesh uses a freemium pricing model: its Pro tier is free for companies with up to three users, including unlimited cards and cashback with no long-term commitment.

Companies that need more advanced spend-control and reporting features can upgrade to the Premium tier at roughly $13 per user per month, while large organizations with complex global spend and travel requirements can get custom Enterprise pricing.

features.title: Real-Time Spend Dashboards

features.title: Travel and Expense Management
features.description: Combines travel booking and related spend management with everyday corporate card controls in one platform.

features.title: Cashback Rewards
features.description: Offers cashback on card transactions across all pricing tiers.

features.title: Vendor and Subscription Management
features.description: Helps track and control recurring vendor and SaaS subscription spend.

pros: Free tier available for small teams, unlike many corporate card competitors
Cardless issuance lets teams provision cards instantly rather than waiting for physical delivery
Combines spend management and travel and expense in a single platform
Real-time visibility into spend helps finance teams catch issues before month-end
Backed by well-known investors, suggesting financial stability at scale
cons: Free tier is capped at just three users, so most growing teams will need a paid plan
Premium tier pricing is per-user, which can add up for larger organizations
Enterprise pricing is custom and not published, requiring a sales conversation
Smaller market presence and brand recognition compared to larger competitors like Brex or Ramp
Some advanced spend-control and reporting features are reserved for higher-priced tiers

faqs.question: What does Mesh Payments do?
faqs.answer: Mesh Payments is a corporate spend management platform offering cardless corporate cards, automated expense management and travel and expense controls for businesses.

faqs.question: Is Mesh Payments free?
faqs.answer: Mesh offers a free Pro tier for companies with up to three users, with a Premium tier at roughly $13 per user per month and custom Enterprise pricing for larger organizations.

faqs.question: Who founded Mesh Payments?
faqs.answer: Mesh Payments was founded in 2018 by Eran Katoni and Oded Zehavi.

faqs.question: Where is Mesh Payments headquartered?
faqs.answer: Mesh Payments is headquartered in New York.

faqs.question: How much funding has Mesh Payments raised?
faqs.answer: Mesh Payments has raised approximately $123 million in total funding from investors including Tiger Global Management, TLV Partners and Alpha Wave Global.

faqs.question: Does Mesh Payments offer travel management?
faqs.answer: Yes, Mesh includes dedicated travel and expense (T&E) management functionality alongside its core corporate card and spend management features.

use_cases.audience: Finance and operations teams managing corporate travel

enrichment.short_description: Metabase is an open-source business intelligence platform that lets teams explore data, build dashboards and share insights without needing to write SQL.
enrichment.seo_title: Metabase Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Metabase review: open-source BI and analytics platform with dashboards, SQL and no-code querying. See features, self-hosted vs cloud pricing and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Metabase is an open-source business intelligence and analytics platform that lets both technical and non-technical users explore data, build charts and dashboards, and share insights, without necessarily needing to write SQL. The project began in 2014 as a side project by Sameer Al-Sakran, together with co-founders including Allen Gilliland and Caspar Strickland, while Al-Sakran was working out of Expa, a San Francisco startup studio; Al-Sakran had previously run a startup called Settle and was motivated by the friction he had experienced getting straightforward answers out of company databases. The team open-sourced an early, AGPL-licensed Java application with a visual query builder that let non-technical users connect to a database and build charts within minutes, and the tool spread largely through GitHub and developer word of mouth rather than paid marketing. Metabase Inc. raised a roughly $2 million seed round led by NEA to polish the user experience and installation flow, followed by an $8 million Series A also led by NEA in 2019, and a $30 million Series B led by Insight Partners in August 2021, funding that helped grow the company to more than 100 employees and build out enterprise sales and customer-success functions; as of 2026 the company employs roughly 120 to 126 people and is headquartered in San Francisco. Architecturally, Metabase connects to relational databases and data warehouses (PostgreSQL, MySQL, Snowflake, BigQuery, Redshift and others), and offers a no-code visual query builder alongside a native SQL editor for more advanced users, letting a single tool serve both business users asking simple questions and analysts writing complex queries. Core features include interactive dashboards, scheduled email and Slack alerts, embedded analytics for building customer-facing reporting into other products, X-ray automated data exploration that surfaces summary insights automatically, and role-based permissions for governing who can see which data. Metabase is distributed in two license tracks: a free, permissively licensed Open Source edition (self-hosted, unlimited users, community support) and a commercial Enterprise/Pro edition adding SSO, SCIM provisioning, audit logs, advanced permissions and official support. It is also offered as a fully managed Metabase Cloud service, with Starter plans reported at roughly $500 per month including 10 users (plus per-additional-user fees), Pro plans at roughly $575 per month, and custom Enterprise pricing starting around $20,000 per year for the largest deployments. Metabase's target customers range from startups and small data teams self-hosting the free edition to run internal analytics, up to larger companies that pay for Metabase Cloud or Enterprise to get managed hosting, SSO and embedded analytics for customer-facing dashboards. Its main competitors include Looker (Google Cloud), Tableau (Salesforce), Power BI (Microsoft), Apache Superset, Redash and Preset. Metabase differentiates itself through its combination of genuine open-source availability, ease of installation, and a no-code interface accessible to non-technical users, positioning it as a lighter-weight, faster-to-deploy alternative to enterprise BI suites like Tableau or Looker.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Metabase is an open-source business intelligence and analytics platform designed to let both technical and non-technical users ask questions of their data and build dashboards without necessarily writing SQL. It began in 2014 as a side project by Sameer Al-Sakran and co-founders at the Expa startup studio in San Francisco, growing through developer word-of-mouth on GitHub before becoming a funded company.

The company has raised funding from NEA and Insight Partners across seed, Series A and Series B rounds, grown to over 100 employees, and now offers Metabase both as free self-hosted open-source software and as a fully managed Metabase Cloud service.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Metabase connects to popular relational databases and data warehouses such as PostgreSQL, MySQL, Snowflake, BigQuery and Redshift, and provides both a no-code visual query builder for business users and a native SQL editor for analysts, letting one tool serve mixed-skill teams.

The platform includes interactive dashboards, scheduled alerts delivered by email or Slack, embedded analytics for building customer-facing reporting into other products, and an automated 'X-ray' exploration feature that surfaces summary insights about a dataset without manual query-writing.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Metabase's Open Source edition is completely free to self-host, with unlimited users and community support, making it a popular starting point for startups and small data teams.

For teams wanting managed hosting or enterprise features like SSO and audit logs, Metabase Cloud Starter is priced at roughly $500 per month for 10 users, Cloud Pro at roughly $575 per month, and Enterprise pricing starts around $20,000 per year for the largest deployments.

features.title: No-Code Visual Query Builder
features.description: Lets non-technical users explore data and build charts without writing SQL.

features.title: Native SQL Editor
features.description: Gives analysts and engineers a full SQL editor for more advanced or custom queries.

features.title: Interactive Dashboards
features.description: Combines multiple charts and questions into shareable, interactive dashboards.

features.title: Embedded Analytics
features.description: Lets companies embed Metabase dashboards and charts directly into their own customer-facing products.

features.title: Scheduled Alerts
features.description: Sends automated email or Slack alerts when data crosses defined thresholds.

features.title: X-Ray Automated Exploration
features.description: Automatically generates summary insights and visualizations about a dataset with one click.

features.title: Broad Database Support
features.description: Connects to major relational databases and data warehouses including PostgreSQL, MySQL, Snowflake, BigQuery and Redshift.

features.title: Role-Based Permissions
features.description: Governs which users and groups can view or query specific data, available in more depth on paid tiers.

pros: Free, genuinely open-source edition with unlimited users and no artificial feature caps on core functionality
Easy to install and self-host, running from a single JAR file or Docker container
Accessible no-code interface makes it usable by non-technical business users, not just analysts
Broad support for popular databases and data warehouses out of the box
Large, active open-source community with strong GitHub presence and frequent releases
cons: Enterprise features such as SSO, SCIM and audit logs require a paid Pro or Enterprise plan
Self-hosted deployments require the user to manage servers, upgrades and backups
Cloud Starter and Pro plans can get expensive quickly as user counts grow beyond included seats
Less advanced modeling and governance tooling than large enterprise BI suites like Looker or Tableau
Enterprise tier pricing is not fully transparent and requires a custom quote

faqs.question: Is Metabase free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Metabase's Open Source edition is free to self-host with unlimited users; paid Cloud and Enterprise tiers add managed hosting, SSO and advanced governance features.

faqs.question: Who created Metabase?
faqs.answer: Metabase was started in 2014 by Sameer Al-Sakran along with co-founders including Allen Gilliland, originally as a side project at the Expa startup studio in San Francisco.

faqs.question: Does Metabase require SQL knowledge?
faqs.answer: No, Metabase includes a no-code visual query builder for non-technical users, alongside a native SQL editor for analysts who prefer to write queries directly.

faqs.question: What databases does Metabase support?
faqs.answer: Metabase connects to major relational databases and data warehouses including PostgreSQL, MySQL, Snowflake, BigQuery and Redshift, among others.

faqs.question: How much does Metabase Cloud cost?
faqs.answer: Metabase Cloud Starter is priced at roughly $500 per month including 10 users, with Pro plans at roughly $575 per month and custom Enterprise pricing above that.

faqs.question: Can Metabase be embedded in another product?
faqs.answer: Yes, Metabase supports embedded analytics, letting companies embed dashboards and charts directly into their own customer-facing applications.

use_cases.title: Internal Company Analytics
use_cases.description: Startups and small data teams self-host the free Open Source edition to give employees self-service dashboards and reporting.

use_cases.title: Embedded Customer-Facing Analytics
use_cases.description: SaaS companies use Metabase's embedding features to build analytics dashboards directly into their own product for customers.

use_cases.title: Enterprise Governed Reporting
use_cases.description: Larger organizations use Metabase Cloud or Enterprise for managed hosting, SSO and permission-governed reporting across departments.
